Explain what a scheduler does in an operating system.
A scheduler decides which runnable thread or process gets CPU time next. It balances responsiveness, fairness, throughput, and latency according to the policy the kernel uses.
What is a context switch?

A context switch saves one execution context and restores another so the CPU can resume a different thread. It preserves registers, stack pointer, and program counter, and on many systems flushes or tags the TLB.
Why can a use-after-free bug be dangerous?

Freed memory can be reused for unrelated data, so a use-after-free may read or corrupt that data, crash, or become an exploitable vulnerability. It is worst when the stale pointer is used for a write or a virtual call.
Describe a page fault.

A page fault fires when code touches a virtual address that is not mapped or not permitted. The kernel resolves it by loading the page, growing the stack/heap, doing copy-on-write, or killing the faulting process.
What is virtual memory?
Virtual memory gives each process a private address space the kernel maps to physical frames. It provides isolation, protection, demand paging, and lets processes share pages safely.
What is the difference between a trap and an interrupt?

A trap is synchronous, caused by the current instruction (syscall, fault, breakpoint). An interrupt is asynchronous, raised by external hardware or a timer. Both transfer control to privileged handler code.
Why are guard pages useful?
A guard page is left unmapped so any overflow into it faults immediately, catching stack overruns or buffer overflows close to the source instead of silently corrupting neighboring memory.
What is stack unwinding?

Unwinding walks active call frames using saved frame pointers or unwind metadata (.eh_frame / .pdata). Debuggers, crash reporters, and exception handling use it to run destructors or build a backtrace.
When should you use RAII?
Whenever a resource must be released reliably: memory, file handles, locks, sockets, GPU objects. Tying acquisition to an object's lifetime makes cleanup automatic and exception-safe.
Why is move semantics important in C++?
Moves transfer ownership of expensive resources (heap buffers, handles) instead of deep-copying them. This cuts allocations and copies and enables ownership-aware types like unique_ptr and vector.
What problem does std::span solve?
span is a non-owning view over a contiguous range that carries both pointer and length. It lets you pass array/buffer slices without copying and without losing size information.
Why consider Rust for systems code?
Rust eliminates whole classes of memory-safety bugs at compile time via ownership and borrowing, while keeping low-level control and no GC. It shines where safety and performance both matter.
When is unsafe Rust justified?

When you need something the safe subset cannot express: FFI, raw hardware access, custom memory layouts, or a proven-correct optimization. Keep it minimal, wrapped in a safe API, and heavily reviewed.
What is a render graph?
A render graph models a frame as passes with explicit resource dependencies. From that graph the engine can auto-order passes, insert barriers, alias transient memory, and cull unused work.
Why use explicit APIs like Vulkan or D3D12?
They hand synchronization, memory management, and command recording to the application, enabling multithreaded submission and predictable performance, at the cost of much more explicit bookkeeping.
What is double buffering?
Two buffers alternate: one is displayed while the other is drawn, then they swap. It removes tearing from mid-scanout writes and lets the producer and consumer overlap.
Explain frame pacing.
Frame pacing is how evenly frames are delivered over time. Consistent intervals feel smoother than a higher but jittery average FPS, because the eye notices variance as stutter.
What is a physics broadphase?
A cheap first pass (grid, sweep-and-prune, or BVH) that discards pairs that cannot collide, shrinking the set the expensive narrowphase must test exactly.
How do you isolate a heisenbug?
Increase determinism: pin threads, reduce concurrency, fix seeds, add tracing, and shrink the reproducer by binary elimination. A reliable repro is 90 percent of the fix.
What is the value of structured logging?
Logging typed key-value fields makes events queryable and correlatable across services, so you can aggregate, filter, and trace a request end-to-end instead of grepping free text.
How do you design a good error message?
Say what failed, where, and what the caller can do next. Include the offending value and context, avoid blame, and make it actionable rather than just descriptive.
Why are invariants important?
Invariants are conditions that must always hold. Naming them gives you precise targets for asserts and tests and makes code far easier to reason about locally.
What is property-based testing?
Instead of fixed cases, you assert properties that must hold for many generated inputs (e.g. encode then decode is identity). It finds edge cases hand-written tests miss.
Why use small commits?
Small, focused commits are easier to review, revert, and bisect, and they record intent clearly. Large mixed commits hide bugs and make history hard to reason about.
What makes a good pull request?
One focused change, an explanation of what and why, and tests that prove it. Unrelated work is split out so review stays fast and the diff is legible.
How do you use git bisect effectively?
Give it a known-good and known-bad revision and a script that returns pass/fail, and it binary-searches to the first bad commit. Automate the test so long histories converge fast.
Why keep CI fast?
Fast CI tightens the feedback loop so people run it often and trust it. Slow pipelines get skipped, batched, or ignored, which lets regressions slip in.
What is a code review looking for?
Correctness, edge cases, clarity, maintainability, and whether the change matches intent. It also guards against architectural drift and shares context across the team.
How do you think about optimizing a game loadout?
Start from the mission goal, then pick tools that improve survival, damage, utility, or economy for that goal. Optimization is choosing tradeoffs, not maxing every stat.
Why is synergy more important than raw numbers in a build?
Abilities and mods that reinforce each other compound, often beating a stronger-looking but disconnected set. Synergy turns separate bonuses into a multiplicative effect.
What is opportunity cost in game builds?
It is what you give up by choosing one mod/weapon/ability over another. It explains why a strong-looking upgrade can still be suboptimal for a specific role.
Why are niche tools sometimes better than generalists?
When a mission has a narrow demand (armor strip, crowd control, mobility), a specialist dominates it. Generalists are safer but rarely peak on any single axis.
Why does a cat knead?
Kneading is a comfort behavior rooted in kittenhood nursing. Adult cats knead when relaxed, content, seeking attention, or settling a resting spot.
What does a cat's tail tell you?
Tail posture signals arousal and intent: upright and relaxed reads as confident and friendly, while stiff, low, or thrashing tails signal tension or irritation.
What helps a stressed cat?
Predictable routines, safe hiding spots, gradual introductions, vertical space, and reduced noise. Minimize sudden change and let the cat control its own approach.
What is cache locality and why does it matter?

Locality is accessing memory that is near recent accesses in space or time, so data is already in cache. Contiguous, sequential access can be an order of magnitude faster than pointer-chasing due to fewer cache misses.
Explain false sharing.

False sharing is when two threads write different variables that happen to sit on the same cache line, so the line ping-pongs between cores. Padding hot per-thread data to separate cache lines fixes it.
What is a data race?

A data race is two threads accessing the same memory concurrently with at least one write and no synchronization. It is undefined behavior in C++ and Rust; use atomics, locks, or ownership to order the accesses.
When would you choose a lock-free queue?
When contention is high and you cannot tolerate a thread blocking others (real-time audio, low-latency systems). Lock-free is harder to get right, so reserve it for measured hotspots, not by default.
What does memory_order_acquire/release mean?

Release on a store and acquire on the matching load create a happens-before edge: everything before the release is visible to code after the acquire. It is cheaper than sequential consistency while still ordering the handoff.
Why prefer composition over inheritance?

Composition assembles behavior from parts you can swap and test independently, avoiding deep fragile hierarchies and the tight coupling of implementation inheritance. Inherit for true is-a, compose for has-a.
What is the rule of zero in C++?

Design types so you never write custom destructors, copy, or move members; let RAII members (unique_ptr, vector) handle resources. If you must write one special member, you usually must consider all five.
When should you pass by value vs const reference in C++?
Pass cheap-to-copy or sink-into types by value (and move), pass large read-only objects by const reference. Passing by value plus std::move can be clearer than reference overloads for constructors.
What causes iterator invalidation?

Operations that reallocate or reorder a container's storage, like vector push_back that grows capacity or erase. Invalidated iterators, pointers, and references must not be used; re-acquire them after the mutation.
Why is undefined behavior dangerous even if it seems to work?
Compilers optimize assuming UB never happens, so code may work today and break with a new optimization level or compiler. UB can silently delete checks, reorder effects, or corrupt memory.
What is the difference between latency and throughput?
Latency is time to complete one operation; throughput is operations completed per unit time. Batching and pipelining raise throughput but often add latency; tune for whichever the workload needs.
What is amortized complexity?

The average cost per operation across a sequence, even if some individual operations are expensive. A vector's push_back is amortized O(1) because occasional O(n) reallocations are spread over many cheap inserts.
When is a hash map the wrong choice?

When you need ordering, range queries, or worst-case guarantees, or when keys are few and small (a flat array or sorted vector may be faster and more cache-friendly). Hashing also has collision and resize costs.
What is a bloom filter for?

A space-efficient probabilistic set that answers definitely-not-present or maybe-present. It is used to skip expensive lookups (disk, network) when most queries are misses; it never has false negatives, only false positives.
Explain big-O vs real performance.

Big-O describes growth as input scales, not constant factors or memory behavior. A lower-complexity algorithm can lose to a simpler one on small or cache-friendly inputs; always measure at realistic sizes.
What is TCP head-of-line blocking?
TCP delivers bytes in order, so a single lost packet stalls all later data until it is retransmitted. It hurts multiplexed protocols like HTTP/2 over TCP; QUIC avoids it with independent streams.
Why does TCP use a three-way handshake?
SYN, SYN-ACK, ACK let both sides agree on initial sequence numbers and confirm each direction works before sending data. It establishes reliable, ordered, connection state on both ends.
What is backpressure in a system?
Backpressure is signaling upstream to slow down when a consumer cannot keep up, preventing unbounded queues and memory blowups. Bounded buffers, credits, or blocking are common mechanisms.
Why is idempotency important in APIs?
An idempotent operation can be retried safely with the same result, which matters because networks drop responses. Clients can resend without duplicating side effects like double charges.
What does eventual consistency mean?
Replicas may temporarily disagree but converge to the same state once updates stop. It trades immediate consistency for availability and partition tolerance, and requires conflict resolution rules.
Explain the CAP theorem simply.

During a network partition a distributed store must choose between consistency (reject or delay) and availability (serve possibly-stale data). Outside partitions you can have both; CAP is about the partition case.
What is a database index really doing?

An index is an auxiliary ordered structure (usually a B-tree) mapping key values to row locations, so lookups and range scans avoid full table scans. It speeds reads but costs storage and slows writes.
When can an index hurt performance?
On write-heavy tables (every insert/update maintains the index), on low-cardinality columns where the optimizer prefers a scan, or when there are so many indexes that writes and planning slow down.
What is an N+1 query problem?
Fetching a list then issuing one query per item (1 + N queries) instead of a single join or batched query. It looks fine at small scale and collapses under real data; fix with joins or eager loading.
Why use prepared statements?
They separate SQL from data, preventing SQL injection and letting the database cache the query plan. Parameters are sent as values, never concatenated into the query text.
What is a deadlock and how do you avoid it?
A deadlock is a cycle of threads each holding a lock the next needs. Avoid it by acquiring locks in a global order, using try-lock with backoff, or reducing lock scope so cycles cannot form.
What is priority inversion?

A high-priority task waits on a lock held by a low-priority task that a medium task keeps preempting, so the high task is effectively blocked. Priority inheritance temporarily boosts the holder to fix it.
Why sample a profiler instead of instrumenting?

Sampling periodically records the call stack with low, uniform overhead and no code changes, giving a statistical view of where time goes. Instrumentation is precise but perturbs the very hotspots it measures.
What is the first rule of optimization?
Measure before you change anything. Profile to find the real hotspot, because intuition about where time goes is usually wrong, and optimizing cold code wastes effort and adds risk.
When should you cache a result?

When it is expensive to compute, reused often, and either stable or acceptably stale. Always plan invalidation and bound the cache size; a cache without an eviction and invalidation story becomes a bug source.
What is cache invalidation and why is it hard?
It is deciding when a cached value is stale and must be recomputed. It is hard because correctness depends on tracking every input that affects the value, and missing one yields silent wrong answers.
What is the difference between a mutex and a semaphore?
A mutex is a lock with ownership: the thread that locks must unlock, protecting a critical section. A semaphore is a counter for signaling or limiting concurrent access, and any thread can signal it.
Why prefer immutable data where practical?
Immutable values cannot be changed after creation, so they are inherently thread-safe, cache-friendly, and easy to reason about. You trade some copying for far fewer aliasing and race bugs.
What is dependency injection for?

It supplies a component's collaborators from outside rather than having it construct them, decoupling usage from creation. That makes code testable (inject fakes) and configurable without editing internals.
When is premature abstraction a problem?
When you generalize before you have two or three real uses, guessing at the axis of variation. The wrong abstraction is costlier than duplication; wait for the pattern to reveal itself.
What is the single responsibility principle really about?
A module should have one reason to change, meaning one cohesive concern. It keeps changes local and reduces the blast radius when requirements shift, not literally 'do one thing'.
Why write the test first sometimes?
Writing the test first forces you to define the interface and expected behavior before implementation, catches unclear requirements early, and guarantees the test actually fails before it passes.
What makes a test flaky and why is that bad?
Flaky tests pass or fail nondeterministically due to timing, ordering, or shared state. They erode trust so people ignore failures, hiding real regressions; fix the nondeterminism or quarantine them.
What is a memory leak in a garbage-collected language?
Objects that are still reachable but never used again, so the GC cannot free them, e.g. an ever-growing cache, listener, or static collection. It is a logic leak, not a missing free.
How does a generational garbage collector work?
It exploits the fact that most objects die young by collecting a small nursery frequently and promoting survivors to older generations collected rarely. This makes the common case cheap.
What is tail-call optimization?

When a function's last action is a call, the compiler can reuse the current stack frame instead of adding a new one, letting deep recursion run in constant stack space. Not all languages guarantee it.
Why is floating point not associative?
Rounding at each step means (a+b)+c can differ from a+(b+c). Order of operations changes results, which is why parallel reductions and compiler reassociation can alter numeric output.
What is a NaN and how does it propagate?

NaN (not-a-number) results from invalid operations like 0/0 or sqrt of a negative. It propagates through arithmetic and compares unequal to everything including itself, which is how you can detect it.
What is fixed-point vs floating-point?

Fixed-point stores a fixed number of fractional bits with integer math, giving deterministic, uniform precision; floating-point trades uniformity for enormous dynamic range. Determinism-sensitive sims often use fixed-point.
Why do networked games use client-side prediction?
To hide latency, the client simulates the local player's actions immediately and reconciles when the authoritative server update arrives. Without it, every input would feel delayed by the round trip.
What is server reconciliation?
After predicting locally, the client replays its unacknowledged inputs on top of the latest authoritative server state to correct any divergence, snapping smoothly rather than teleporting.
Why is an authoritative server used in multiplayer?
It is the single source of truth for game state, so clients cannot cheat by editing their local simulation. Clients send inputs; the server validates and simulates.
What is delta compression in networking?
Sending only what changed since the last acknowledged state instead of the full snapshot. It drastically cuts bandwidth for state that mostly stays the same frame to frame.
What is an entity-component-system (ECS)?
ECS stores data as components in contiguous arrays keyed by entity id, and systems process components in bulk. It favors cache-friendly data layout and composition over deep object hierarchies.
Why does data-oriented design help performance?
It arranges memory around how the CPU actually accesses it, processing arrays of homogeneous data to maximize cache use and enable SIMD, instead of scattered object graphs that thrash the cache.
What is a spatial partition and when do you need one?

A grid, quadtree/octree, or BVH that groups objects by location so queries (nearest, in-range, collision candidates) touch only local objects. You need it once naive all-pairs checks dominate the frame.
How does a depth buffer resolve visibility?
Each pixel stores the nearest depth seen so far; a new fragment is drawn only if it is closer, otherwise discarded. It gives correct per-pixel occlusion without sorting triangles.
What is the difference between forward and deferred rendering?

Forward shades each object against all lights as it draws; deferred writes geometry attributes to a G-buffer, then shades once per pixel over all lights. Deferred scales to many lights but costs bandwidth and complicates transparency and MSAA.
What is mipmapping and why does it help?
Precomputed downscaled versions of a texture; the GPU samples the level matching the pixel's footprint. It removes shimmering aliasing on distant surfaces and improves texture cache locality.
Why prefix a batch of draws to reduce state changes?
Changing shaders, textures, or render targets flushes GPU pipeline state and costs CPU driver overhead. Sorting draws to minimize state switches and batching them keeps both CPU and GPU fed.
What is a symbol table in a compiled binary?

It maps names (functions, globals) to addresses and types. Stripping it shrinks the binary and hinders reverse engineering, since a stripped binary shows only sub_XXXX addresses instead of readable names.
What does a decompiler like Hex-Rays actually do?

It lifts disassembly into an intermediate representation, recovers control flow and types, and emits C-like pseudocode. It is a best-effort reconstruction, so names are guessed and inlined/optimized code can be hard to read.
Why is optimized code harder to reverse engineer?

Optimizations inline functions, hoist and reorder code, vectorize loops with SIMD, and elide variables, so the binary no longer maps cleanly to source structure. The logic is intact but scattered and transformed.
What is a calling convention?
The contract for how functions pass arguments (registers vs stack), return values, and who cleans up (caller vs callee). Getting it wrong when calling across languages or in asm corrupts the stack.
What is ASLR and what does it defend against?

Address space layout randomization randomizes where code and data load, so an attacker cannot hardcode addresses for exploits. It raises the bar for memory-corruption attacks but is not a complete defense.
Why validate input at trust boundaries?

Any data crossing from a less-trusted source (network, files, users) can be malformed or hostile. Validating and canonicalizing it at the boundary prevents injection, overflow, and logic bugs deeper in the system.
What is the principle of least privilege?
Give each component only the permissions it needs, for as short a time as possible. It limits the damage of a compromise or bug, since a breached component cannot exceed its narrow grant.
Why hash and salt passwords instead of encrypting them?

Encryption is reversible with a key that can leak; you never need the original password, only to verify it. A slow salted hash (bcrypt/argon2) makes each guess expensive and defeats precomputed rainbow tables.
What is a race condition in file handling?
A time-of-check-to-time-of-use gap where the file changes between when you check it and when you use it, e.g. a symlink swap. Use atomic operations or open-then-fstat to avoid the window.
What does 'fail closed' mean?
On error or uncertainty, deny access or stop rather than allow. Security-sensitive systems fail closed so a bug or outage cannot silently grant more than intended.
When should you add a feature flag?
When you want to decouple deploy from release, roll out gradually, or kill a risky path fast. Flags add complexity, so give each one an owner and a removal plan to avoid permanent debt.
Why keep functions small and focused?
Short functions are easier to name, test, and reason about, and they compose. If you cannot name a function crisply, it is probably doing too much and should be split.
What is a memory arena / bump allocator?

A block of memory you allocate from by advancing a pointer, freeing everything at once by resetting the pointer. It is extremely fast and fragmentation-free for data with a shared lifetime, like a frame.
Why avoid allocating in a hot loop?
Allocation can lock, fragment the heap, and thrash the cache, and its cost is unpredictable. Preallocate or reuse buffers so the hot path does no heap work and stays cache-resident.
What is SIMD and when does it help?

Single Instruction Multiple Data processes several values per instruction (e.g. 4 floats at once). It helps data-parallel, branch-light work like image, audio, and geometry math, given contiguous, aligned data.
Why is branch misprediction costly?
CPUs speculatively execute the predicted branch; a mispredict flushes the pipeline and wastes the speculative work, costing many cycles. Branchless code or predictable patterns help in tight loops.
What is copy-on-write?

Shared data stays shared until someone writes, at which point a private copy is made. It makes cheap logical copies (fork, string sharing) while preserving isolation, deferring cost until a mutation actually happens.
How do you reason about a memory dump after a crash?

Find the faulting thread and instruction, unwind the stack for context, inspect the arguments and nearby heap/stack, and map addresses to symbols. Look for freed/zeroed pointers, off-by-one indices, and corrupted headers.
Why is determinism valuable in a simulation?

Deterministic sims reproduce exactly from the same inputs and seed, which enables replay, lockstep networking, and debugging of otherwise ephemeral issues. It requires controlling float order, iteration order, and RNG.
What is the difference between concurrency and parallelism?

Concurrency is structuring a program as independently progressing tasks (may interleave on one core); parallelism is actually running work simultaneously on multiple cores. Concurrency is about design, parallelism about execution.
When should you shard data?

When a single node cannot hold the data or serve the load, and access has a natural partition key. Sharding adds cross-shard query and rebalancing complexity, so shard only when vertical scaling runs out.
Why measure p99 latency, not just the average?
Averages hide tail latency; the slowest 1 percent of requests often dominate user-perceived pain and can cascade under load. Tracking p95/p99 exposes the outliers that SLAs and users actually feel.
What is a good default for handling an unexpected error?

Fail loudly and locally: log with context, preserve invariants, and either recover cleanly or propagate a typed error upward. Silently swallowing errors turns a clear failure into a mysterious one later.
How do you approach an unfamiliar codebase?

Find the entry points and build/run it first, then trace one real feature end to end. Read tests for intended behavior, sketch the module boundaries, and change something small to confirm your mental model.
What is the difference between an assertion and error handling?

Assertions catch programmer bugs that should never happen and can be compiled out; error handling deals with expected runtime conditions like bad input or a missing file. Do not use asserts for recoverable errors.
Why decouple policy from mechanism?

Mechanism is the capability (how to do it); policy is the decision (when and which). Separating them lets you change rules without rewriting the machinery, and reuse the machinery under different rules.
